overviews.beginner: The vine is leafless and resting. This is when growers prune, deciding how many buds to leave. More buds means more fruit. Fewer buds means more concentrated flavors.
overviews.intermediate: Sap has retreated to the roots and the vine can withstand temperatures down to -15C. Pruning now determines bud count, which controls yield, canopy density, and fruit concentration.
overviews.advanced: Dormancy is the structural intervention point. Pruning decisions cascade through the entire season: bud count determines shoot number, which determines canopy density, which determines disease pressure, light interception, and microclimate around the fruit.

regionalNotes.note: Cane pruning (Guyot) is gospel here. Skilled pruners are so valued that some domaines employ the same team for decades.
Some Shiraz vines are 150+ years old on their own roots, pre-phylloxera. These gnarly bush vines self-regulate yield naturally.
The most regulated pruning in the wine world. AOC inspectors check bud counts. Even the pruning style is codified by sub-region.
Terraced schist slopes so steep that mules once carried cuttings. Some estates still can't mechanize.

decisions.title: Pruning
decisions.text: Cutting back last year's growth to control how many buds the vine produces. Think of it as setting a budget: the vine has finite energy.

risks.title: Over or under pruning
risks.text: Too few buds wastes the vine's capacity. Too many buds dilutes quality.

decisions.title: Spur vs. cane pruning
decisions.text: Spur pruning cuts to 2-3 bud spurs. Best for Grenache, Syrah. Cane pruning retains 8-12 bud canes for Pinot Noir, Riesling, Chardonnay whose basal buds are often infertile.

decisions.title: Bud count and yield
decisions.text: Cool climates retain more buds for leaf area. Warm climates can ripen with fewer. Appellation laws cap yields: Burgundy Grand Cru at ~35 hl/ha.

risks.title: Trunk disease
risks.text: Every cut is an infection entry. Eutypa and Esca are slow killers that hollow out trunks over years. Once established, there's no cure.

decisions.title: Training system architecture
decisions.text: Guyot separates renewal and fruiting zones. Cordon simplifies labor but risks dead-arm disease. Gobelet maximizes root-to-canopy ratio in hot, dry sites. Divided canopies (Scott Henry, Lyre) split the fruiting zone into two curtains.

decisions.title: Rootstock as a viticultural tool
decisions.text: 110R confers drought tolerance. SO4 promotes vigor on poor soils. 3309C restricts vigor on fertile soils. Riparia Gloire advances ripening by two weeks. The rootstock-scion interaction affects berry size, phenolic development, and wine style.

risks.title: Phylloxera and ungrafted vines
risks.text: Sandy soils and geographic isolation allow ungrafted vinifera to survive. Some believe ungrafted vines produce more complex wine. But the risk is existential.

risks.title: Vine age economics
risks.text: Old vines (40+ years) produce less fruit with more concentration. But they're expensive to maintain. The decision to replant sacrifices 3-5 years of production.

overviews.beginner: As temperatures rise, buds swell and break open. Tiny shoots emerge. This is the most vulnerable time because frost can kill everything overnight.
overviews.intermediate: Bud break begins when soil temperature exceeds 10C. Within weeks, shoots extend and the vine flowers. Frost kills green tissue at roughly -1C. Poor weather during flowering causes fruit-set disorders.
overviews.advanced: The phenological window from bud break through fruit set is the highest-risk period in the annual cycle. Frost probability models, growing degree day accumulation, and variety-specific bud break timing all factor into risk management.

regionalNotes.note: Frost devastated 2016, 2017, 2019, and 2021 here. Many growers now maintain permanent aspersion infrastructure costing 15,000+ euros per hectare.
Spring frost at 1,000m+ altitude is managed by site selection. The Zonda wind can raise temperatures 15C in hours.
Over 200 wind machines now operate across the region, their drone audible on cold spring nights.
The world's southernmost wine region at 45S. Frost risk extends into November.

decisions.title: Frost protection
decisions.text: Green tissue dies at roughly -1C. Growers choose between heaters, wind machines, sprinklers, or accepting the risk.

risks.title: Late frost
risks.text: A single freezing night after bud break can kill young shoots and destroy part or all of the crop.

decisions.title: Frost protection methods
decisions.text: Heaters raise air 1-3C (radiation frost only). Wind machines pull warm inversion-layer air down (~30,000 euros per unit). Aspersion uses latent heat from freezing water (334 J/g) to hold buds at 0C.

risks.title: Coulure and millerandage
risks.text: Cold or wet flowering causes poor pollination. Coulure drops flowers without setting fruit. Millerandage produces mixed-size berries that can paradoxically increase concentration.

decisions.title: Frost type diagnosis
decisions.text: Radiation frost (clear, still, inversion layer present): wind machines, heaters, and aspersion all work. Advection frost (moving cold air mass, no inversion): only aspersion is reliable.

decisions.title: Site selection as defense
decisions.text: Mid-slope sites drain cold air to valley floors. Proximity to rivers provides thermal mass. Many great vineyard sites were selected centuries ago precisely because local farmers knew where frost pooled.

risks.title: Climate change phenology mismatch
risks.text: Bud break has advanced by 2-3 weeks across European vineyards since 1980, but last-frost dates have shifted by only 1 week. The vine wakes up into a more dangerous window.

overviews.beginner: Shoots grow fast, leaves unfurl, and the canopy fills in. Too much canopy creates shade and disease. Growers open it up to let sunlight and air reach the fruit.
overviews.intermediate: Canopy management is the ongoing work of balancing the vine's solar panel (leaves) against the risk of shading and disease.
overviews.advanced: The canopy is a photosynthetic engine. Optimal leaf area to fruit weight ratio is roughly 10-15 cm2 per gram of fruit. The Dr. Richard Smart canopy management revolution of the 1980s demonstrated that training system and canopy architecture often matter more than terroir.

regionalNotes.note: Maritime humidity means spraying 8-15 times per season in conventional viticulture. The cost of going organic here is genuinely heroic.
Slopes of 65 degrees make everything manual. Per-hectare labor costs are among the world's highest, which is why great Mosel Riesling at 15 euros is one of wine's greatest bargains.
Almost zero summer rain means minimal disease pressure. The challenge is managing vigor from deep, fertile valley-floor soils.
The Gimblett Gravels has stony, free-draining soils that naturally restrict vigor. The stones absorb and radiate heat.

decisions.title: Canopy management
decisions.text: Removing excess shoots and leaves to let sunlight and air reach the fruit. Helps grapes ripen evenly and prevents fungal disease.

risks.title: Fungal disease
risks.text: Dense, humid canopies breed downy mildew, powdery mildew, and botrytis. These are the most common vineyard diseases worldwide.

decisions.title: Shoot thinning vs. hedging
decisions.text: Shoot thinning removes excess shoots by hand (40-80 hours/ha). Mechanical hedging is faster but blunt, and cut tips regrow as laterals.

decisions.title: Leaf pulling
decisions.text: Removing leaves around the fruit zone. Improves airflow and dries clusters faster after rain. Too much exposure in heat causes sunburn.

risks.title: Powdery vs. downy mildew
risks.text: Powdery thrives in shade and moderate humidity. Downy needs water on leaf surfaces. Sulfur controls powdery, copper controls downy.

decisions.title: Integrated pest management
decisions.text: IPM combines biological controls, cultural practices, and targeted chemical intervention only when monitoring thresholds are exceeded.

decisions.title: Organic, biodynamic, and regenerative
decisions.text: Organic prohibits synthetics. Biodynamic adds preparations and lunar timing. Regenerative focuses on soil carbon and biodiversity. Each philosophy produces excellent wine.

risks.title: Copper accumulation
risks.text: Organic viticulture relies on copper for downy mildew. But copper accumulates in topsoil, eventually reaching toxic levels. EU caps copper at 4 kg/ha/year.

overviews.beginner: Veraison is when red grapes turn purple and white grapes turn golden. The vine is now focused entirely on ripening. Sugar goes up, acid goes down.
overviews.intermediate: Veraison marks the shift from vegetative to reproductive physiology. Sugar accumulates via photosynthesis, malic acid is consumed through respiration (faster in heat), and phenolic compounds develop in skins.
overviews.advanced: Veraison initiates a cascade of biochemical changes mediated by abscisic acid (ABA) signaling. Anthocyanin biosynthesis begins in red varieties. Methoxypyrazine concentration drops as berry temperature increases. Diurnal temperature variation is the single most discussed climate metric for quality.

regionalNotes.note: Nebbiolo is one of the last varieties to ripen, with veraison in mid-August and harvest sometimes in late October.
The Cape Doctor wind blows fiercely during ripening, drying canopies and concentrating flavors.
Llicorella soils force roots 20 meters deep for water. Yields are naturally tiny, often under 20 hl/ha.
Cool Oregon climate means veraison can start as late as early August. Vintage variation here is dramatic.

decisions.title: Green harvest
decisions.text: Dropping unripe clusters (20-40%) so the vine concentrates energy into fewer bunches.

risks.title: Uneven ripening
risks.text: Not all clusters ripen at the same rate. Good canopy work earlier prevents this.

decisions.title: Deficit irrigation
decisions.text: Applying less water than needed creates mild stress. The vine prioritizes fruit over growth, concentrating flavors.

decisions.title: Tracking ripeness
decisions.text: Measured in Brix, Baume, or Oechsle. 22 Brix = ~12.5% ABV. 24 Brix = ~14%. Growers taste berries daily.

risks.title: Heat spikes
risks.text: Sustained temperatures above 35C shut down photosynthesis. Ripening stops while sugar concentrates through dehydration.

decisions.title: Phenolic ripeness assessment
decisions.text: Sugar ripeness is easy to measure. Phenolic ripeness is harder: seed color, seed crunch, skin tannin texture, pulp release. Experienced growers walk rows daily, chewing berries.

decisions.title: Water stress management
decisions.text: Predawn leaf water potential (measured with pressure chamber) is the gold standard. Values above -0.2 MPa indicate no stress. -0.4 to -0.6 is ideal for quality.

risks.title: Sugar-phenolic mismatch
risks.text: As temperatures rise, sugar accumulates faster while phenolic development can't keep pace. The result: 15%+ potential alcohol while tannins remain green.

overviews.beginner: Sugar is climbing, acid is dropping. Every day adds ripeness but risks weather and rot. When to pick is the most important decision of the year.
overviews.intermediate: The harvest decision integrates sugar, acid, phenolic maturity, weather forecasts, disease pressure, and winery capacity.
overviews.advanced: Harvest timing is a multivariate optimization problem balancing sugar accumulation rate, acid degradation curve, phenolic maturation trajectory, Botrytis infection probability, and precipitation forecast confidence intervals.

regionalNotes.note: Harvest can extend into December for Eiswein. Workers pick frozen berries at -7C by headlamp at 4 AM.
Noble rot requires up to eight selective passes over 4-8 weeks, picking individual berries at the perfect botrytis stage.
Night harvesting by machine at 2-5 AM is standard. Fruit arrives cold, preserving aromatics.
In great years the entire Cote d'Or mobilizes within a 10-day window.

decisions.title: When to pick
decisions.text: Too early gives underripe, tart wine. Too late risks rain, rot, and loss of freshness. Most growers taste berries daily.

risks.title: Rain at harvest
risks.text: Berries absorb rainwater, swelling and diluting concentration. Heavy rain splits skins and invites rot.

decisions.title: Hand picking vs. machine
decisions.text: Hand picking allows selective harvesting. Essential for steep slopes and premium wine. Machine harvesters are fast and cheap but can't sort.

decisions.title: Selective picking
decisions.text: Top estates pick the ripest rows first, return days later for the rest. Each pick is vinified separately.

risks.title: Botrytis: grey rot vs. noble rot
risks.text: Same fungus, opposite outcomes. Persistent humidity on ripe fruit causes grey rot. Morning fog followed by warm sun produces noble rot.

decisions.title: Parcel-level optimization
decisions.text: Top estates monitor 20-50 individual parcels with separate tracking, picking each at its optimal window. The final wine may blend 30+ fermentations.

decisions.title: Sorting technology
decisions.text: Optical sorting machines analyze individual berries at 3-6 tons per hour. They reject based on size, color, and density.

risks.title: Compressed harvest windows
risks.text: Climate change is pushing harvest earlier and compressing the gap between varieties ripening. Some regions now face all varieties ripening within 2 weeks.

beginner.q: You're pruning Pinot Noir in Burgundy. Why choose cane pruning?
options: Pinot Noir's basal buds are often infertile
beginner.why: Pinot Noir's basal buds often produce shoots but no fruit. Cane pruning retains buds further along where fruitfulness is reliable.

beginner.q: A frost warning has been issued. Which method uses latent heat?
beginner.why: Freezing water releases 334 J/g of latent heat, holding bud temperature at 0C. The ice cocoon protects the green tissue.

options: When grapes change color and begin ripening
beginner.why: Veraison is the onset of ripening. Reds turn purple, whites turn gold. Sugar accumulates, acid drops.
Open canopy lets sunlight reach fruit for even ripening and allows air circulation that prevents fungal disease.

beginner.q: Main risk of waiting too long to harvest?
beginner.why: Extra hang time risks rain (dilution), botrytis (rot), and acid loss. Sugar climbs but freshness disappears.

options: Dropping clusters to concentrate remaining fruit
beginner.why: Removing 20-40% of unripe clusters so the vine channels energy into fewer, more concentrated bunches.

intermediate.q: Clear, still night in Chablis, -3C. What frost type?
intermediate.why: Clear, still nights where heat radiates from the ground create radiation frost with a warm inversion layer above.

intermediate.q: Barossa grower wants to concentrate flavors. Which technique?
intermediate.why: RDI supplies less water than needed, creating mild stress. The vine prioritizes fruit, concentrating flavors.

intermediate.q: Dense canopy after spring rain. Which disease concerns you most?
intermediate.why: Powdery mildew thrives in shaded, moderately humid canopies. Canopy opening is the primary defense.

intermediate.q: Why might a Mosel grower leave more buds than a Barossa grower?
options: Cool climates need more leaf area to ripen
intermediate.why: Cool climates need more leaves to photosynthesize enough sugar to ripen fruit with limited sunlight.

intermediate.q: 24 Brix but seeds are green and crunchy. What's the problem?
intermediate.why: Classic warm-climate mismatch. Sugar accumulates faster than tannins mature.
Pickers assess each cluster, taking only ripe, healthy fruit across multiple passes.

advanced.q: Cold, wet flowering produces mixed-size berries. What is this?
advanced.why: Millerandage from poor pollination. Small seedless berries have very high skin-to-juice ratio, paradoxically increasing concentration.

advanced.q: Vineyard at 42N, 800m elevation in Spain. Why cool-climate style?
advanced.why: At 800m, roughly 4.8C cooler than sea level. Enough to shift warm latitude into moderate or cool territory.

advanced.q: Mistral blows during spring frost. Why are wind machines useless?
options: No warm inversion layer in advection frost
advanced.why: Advection frost from the Mistral has no warm inversion layer above. The entire air column is cold. Nothing to mix.

advanced.q: Why did phylloxera devastate European but not American vines?
advanced.why: American species co-evolved with phylloxera, forming cork-like callus at feeding sites. Vinifera has no such defense.

advanced.q: Morning fog then warm sun in Sauternes creates conditions for what?
advanced.why: Fog provides humidity for Botrytis infection. Warm afternoon sun desiccates berries instead of allowing grey rot.

advanced.q: Preparation 501 in biodynamics. What does it claim to enhance?
advanced.why: Ground quartz sprayed on canopy. Claimed to enhance light metabolism. Practiced by DRC, Leroy, Zind-Humbrecht.
